text: Ever have a movie experience so epic
that you just know you're never going to
see anything quite like it again? Like
you'll never quite reach that same
emotional high you felt in that special
moment? Well, probably not so much in
recent years, but by golly, there was
once a time when going to a movie
theater meant a chance to experience
pure magic and wonder on the biggest
screen imaginable. And I don't think any
movies captured that feeling quite like
The Lord of the Rings, Peter Jackson's
Magnum Opus, the epic trilogy to end all
trilogies, the apex of big budget
Hollywood filmm balanced out by mature
storytelling and an abiding respect for
the source material instead of some
narcissistic filmmaker determined to put
their own stamp on it. Films that would
be literally impossible to make at any
other time before or since. And because
the trilogy is getting its 25th
anniversary re-release, oh my god, I'm
old. People have been taking to social
media and sharing some of their favorite
moments and scenes that have stuck with
them for the past quarter of a century.
And well, I felt like it's damn well
time I chimed in with mine because it's
all too easy to spend your days bitching
and moaning about the state of modern
entertainment here on YouTube. Rightly
so in most cases, but it's also nice to
be reminded once in a while that yes,
movies and TV shows can conjure up some
pretty [ __ ] incredible experiences.
And well, this right here is mine.
[screaming]
>> This may be one of the greatest and most
epic scenes not just in the Lord of the
Rings trilogy, not just in fantasy, but
in all of cinema. It is [ __ ]
perfection. the buildup, the stakes, the
character moments, the choreography, the
soundtrack, the culmination of every
story thread and character arc and
decision that's been woven throughout
the whole movie. This is payoff on the
grandest scale imaginable. And watching
this moment unfold in a cinema screen 24
years ago was an experience that will
stay with me for the rest of my life.
And so I want to try to explain why I
think it works so well. Because a great
scene is more than just what happens in
that one moment. It's everything that
led up to it. everyone involved in it,
everything it means for the wider story.
So, let us explore it together, shall
we? First, the setup. The scene takes
place at the culmination of the battle
of Helm's Deep at the end of the Two
Towers, which by the way is my favorite
battle of the trilogy, but that's a
story for another day. Anyway, the
forces of Rohan, led by King Theodin,
are under siege by an army of Urukhai
sent by Saraman to wipe them out. With
most of their horsemen dispersed across
the country, Theodin has no choice but
to lead his depleted forces to the
fortress of Helms Deep, which should at
least give them a fighting chance
against the vastly superior army. As
Aragorn and the others head to the
refuge, Gandalf leaves in search of
Aamir, who led most of his men away
after being dismissed by Worm Tongue.
It's established very clearly that
Theodan and his men are completely
outclassed in this battle. Their army
consists mostly of old men and boys
conscripted into service, hastily armed
with ancient weapons and dented armor.
Relics of past wars barely fit for
purpose. Now they know there's no real
hope of victory. All they can really do
is hold out as long as possible and hope
that Gandalf can get to them in time. On
the other hand, there's the army of
Urukai. Massive, powerful, overwhelming
in number, and composed of the biggest,
strongest, and most fearless orcs the
world has ever seen. an enemy that burns
and destroys everything in its path.
That doesn't care about casualties and
absolutely won't stop until it achieves
its mission. This isn't an army of
invasion or conquest. It's an army of
annihilation. If Helm's Deep falls,
Rohan will die. As far as stakes go, it
doesn't get much higher than that.
Secondly, the characters. Theodan is a
man with the weight of the world resting
on his shoulders. an old king in his
twilight years whose mind was slowly
poisoned by his chief adviser who
allowed his kingdom to fall into decline
and his armies to fade away. Whose own
son was killed in the fighting without
him even knowing about it. A man who was
barely saved by Gandalf who wakes up at
last only to find his country under
siege and the fate of his people
balanced on a knife edge. He knows that
his time is almost up, that this might
be his last battle, but he's determined
to go down fighting. Balancing him out
is Aragorn, a man who struggled to lead
the fellowship ever since Gandalf fell
in Moria, whose every decision seems to
have led to disaster, and who barely
even made it here himself. Just as this
could be Theodin's last battle, it's
also Aragorn's first. He's never led an
army into battle before. He's never
commanded troops or issued orders, but
even he knows that their position is
almost hopeless. Again, though, just
like Theodin, he's resolved to fight
until the bitter ends.
>> Then I shall die as one of them.
Thirdly, there's the battle itself. The
Urukai throw themselves against the
fortress like a wave. This isn't a
carefully coordinated assault with
precise maneuvering and tactical
deployments. It is a brute force
sledgehammer attack that relies on two
things. Overwhelming power and sheer
weight of numbers. JRR Tolken lived
through the horrors of the First World
War. And he saw for himself the human
wave attacks that sacrificed hundreds of
thousands of lives to gain a few miles
of territory. And this battle is a
perfect reflection of that. Bit by bit,
the exhausted and wounded defenders are
forced back into the citadel. The orcs
have taken thousands of casualties by
this point, but it doesn't matter to
them. They don't care about losses. All
they care about is victory, and it's
almost in their grasp.
>> What can men do against such reckless
hate?
>> This is an enemy the likes of which
Theodin and his people have never faced
before. An army that will not stop, that
can't be daunted and won't show them any
mercy. A relentless machine of war that
even human courage can't stand against.
With all hope seemingly lost, Aragorn
suggests one last suicidal charge,
trying to buy time for the women and
children to escape, knowing they won't
make it far, but determined to try
anyway.
>> Ride out and meet them
>> for death and glory for Rohan. You know,
people often point to Theodin's speech
at the Battle of the Pelenor Fields as
the best one of the trilogy, but for me,
it's this one right here.
>> Fell deeds awake.
>> Now for wrath, now for ruin and the Red
Dawn.
>> It's not eloquent or heroic. It doesn't
inspire thoughts of victory. It's the
last words of a man facing the end,
knowing there'll be no one left to sing
songs about him when it's all over. A
man whose only goal now is to fight and
die.
>> And fight they do. Their charge carrying
them all the way down the causeway into
the very heart of the enemy army where
they're sure to be overwhelmed and wiped
out in a matter of seconds. That's when
it finally happens. The thing Aragorn
had pinned all his hopes on. The thing
we as the audience had all but forgotten
about in the chaos and disasters of the
battle.
[ __ ] yeah, bro. And so we come down to
it at last. Gandalf arriving at the last
moment to save the day. With the sun
rising behind him, Aomir and his men
ride into the aid of their king. And
between them, an army of orcs, battered
and depleted after a whole night of
bloody carnage, but still very much in
the fight and determined to win at any
cost. Even now, the situation seems
hopeless. The music you're hearing in
this moment isn't triumphant, it's
tragic. It's preparing you for a brave
but futile last stand. The riders of
Rohan seem to be charging straight into
a wall of spears, hurling themselves
towards certain death. Horses, swords,
and human courage thrown helplessly
against a machine of war. And I say
machine because that's exactly what
they're up against. Pay attention to the
Urukai here. The way they march in lock
step, their brutal industrial looking
armor, the sound their boots make as
they trample the ground.
That is the sound of machinery right
there. And it fits in perfectly with the
experiences Tolken drew from when he
wrote these scenes. World War I was the
first truly industrialized war, the
first war of mass production, where
machinery and technology was more
powerful than human courage. It left him
with a deep mistrust of technology and
industry and the destruction of nature
and tradition. And you can see that
woven throughout his writing and it's
perfectly encapsulated in this scene
right here. And at the last second when
the two armies are about to clash, it
turns out that nature itself lends a
helping hand. Again, people point to the
ride of the Rahim in Return of the King
as their greatest moment in the trilogy.
But for me, it's this. This scene takes
you on the kind of emotional roller
coaster that few other movies have ever
been able to match. Even watching it and
describing it right here brings a lump
to my throat because it's that [ __ ]
good. In a trilogy bursting at the seams
with poignant, epic, awe inspiring
moments, this for me is the greatest one
of them all. Even now, a quarter of a
century later, this scene still gets to
me and I'm very grateful I got to see it
when it happens.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
